sentences.text: Yes.
I-- When I finished sixth grade, I had the highest grades in the whole city, and I received an award.
I was chosen student of the year, and I got award from the-- they call it not mayor, but president of our city, Dr.
[INAUDIBLE].
And I have even the book.
My-- my friend preserved this.
I have it someplace upstairs.
And this was also one more thing why I was accepted to the school, that I was the best student in the city.
No, I was too young for it.
But I was, of course, a Girl Scout.
Girl Scout.
And I was even what we call [POLISH], which means I had a different color of here, some emblems and whatever, and I was leading the whole group.
And that made me very, very happy.
Just before the war, they were actually preparing for war.

And we, in school, were learning also some form organization to which you had to belong, and they told us what to do when there is-- when the German will put gas or something, with gas masks, everything.
So we had, they call it [SPEAKING POLISH].
And we are preparing, actually.
And we had training like soldiers, although we're very young.
Well, in the very beginning was, for example, it affected me a lot because this section of the city was not only made what they call judenrein, but also all the gentile people had to move out.
It was made into a German section.
Now, my mother's brother, who lived in the same house, stayed, because he was number one tailor in the city.
And the governor, Hans Frank, ask who is the best.
And so my uncle was making suits and whatever for him.
And he got permission to stay, actually.
But we had to go out.
And we couldn't take-- our things, yes-- but not furniture or anything of this sort.
So we moved to grandfather's house.
And that was one thing.
And of course, second thing was that I couldn't go to school anymore, which-- I loved school, and the principal said if I have some friends, let them bring me homework.
I had all the books.
I can study.
And that what happened.
Then very soon, they start preparing for ghetto, to put all the Jews in ghetto.
And they picked the worst part of the city with the oldest houses.
But Jews could live.

So since my grandfather had still property outside of the city, we went there, except grandfather, knowing that this will be taken away, it's going to be Jewish, went to a lawyer who put date three weeks before the war, and he gave it to a mayor of that little town.
And when we came in, the guy gave us half of the house, which the whole thing anyway was my grandfather.
And he opened it, and he said, stay with us.
And we stayed there until we had to move to ghetto.
Nobody could stay over there.
We-- now, the funny part was that actually, my grandfather was an American citizen because his father was a citizen.
He was here, and his brother.
Now, my father's family, his much older brother and two sister, were also here.
And we had the papers, which they didn't recognize.
Some people were buying themself paper which were recognized.
I don't know why ours weren't.
But that was it.
So we had to move.
And when we moved from Cracow to the suburbs, we could take some furniture with us, although that was already not ours.
That was grandfather's, because our furniture stayed over there on [POLISH].
And then when we went from that place, when we had to go to ghetto, we took very little because we couldn't get apartments anymore, only a-- a part of a room in-- in some apartments.
So it was-- slowly, slowly they were ruining us completely.
Well, I first [INAUDIBLE], the first thing which I took were my books.
That was very precious to me.
And of course, photographs.
And then we took beds, mattresses, things like this.
Not-- not much more.
In ghetto, my mother's two girlfriend, who also moved together, took us in with a little bit of extra furniture, but not much.
And that was the house on [POLISH], where I also-- where right across in another apartment lived my future husband, with his mother and brother.
Well, my mother's-- Teresa's-- the one got different-- she got, actually, an apartment with her husband.
And then my mother's two sisters and grandfather.
Some of the family was different town.
And we moved, my mother and I, with her two friends.
One of them lived with her four-years-old daughter, and the other one with her father.
It was only two rooms, but very, very large.
It was actually a huge kitchen.
You know, apartments in Europe were much bigger.
Rooms were bigger than United States.
And the bedroom, living room, whatever, dining room, was quite large.
And-- but only two rooms.
And there was no bathroom.
Bathroom was in the foyer.
And this bathroom was for three families, actually.
And no bathtub.
We bought something we could take a bath.
But there was no bathroom.
Well, six.
Six of us.
Big rooms, but still.
Six people.
I mean, two children and four adults.
[SIGHS] There were no rationing whatsoever.
You have a little money, you could buy some food in ghetto.
Now, we were in the position that my mother was once in a while selling a ring or something.
And-- and also she was, what I think, all those applications for different thing.
Now I, believe it or not, I was making a little money by being a dressmaker.
And since I was very skillful, I was making a little money.
So we were not-- unlike very many other people, we were not destitute.
Now, my grandfather, who had money because he and one of his daughter, my aunt, were collecting gold money.
So they had $20 in gold, francs in gold, and so on.
And once they were selling one piece, they could live.
But whoever had some money, they were coming at nighttime and at that time already, taking them to Auschwitz, although still not throwing people out.
So grandfather, who was at that time 84 years old-- was-- there was not much coal to warm the apartment, but there was wood.
He was chopping wood, actually, at this age, because he was so afraid that somebody will say he has reputation as being wealthy.
So he said to everybody, well, I lost my business.
I lost my income from houses.
I lost everything.
I have nothing.
They did not know that he still had a lot of jewelry and those gold pieces, which were a lot of them.
And so he was actually going-- at his age-- he didn't look his age.
He had 20/20 vision, no wrinkles.
Well, his great grandfather lived to be 113, so he had some good genes.
But it was heartbreaking when I saw my grandfather going from house to house and chopping wood.
And it-- it was-- I felt somehow, I don't know, I felt degraded.
And I felt bad about it.
Well, my two aunts, which were very brilliant people.
They were still with us in ghetto.
And they were business women, and very good ones.
And one of them, my mother's sister, was a mathematical genius.
She could multiply and divide large number in her head.
And was very, very brilliant woman.
And seeing that she had no job.
She doesn't have-- she didn't have my mother's skills.
She couldn't type or do steno, which were a good thing.
Incidentally, women, for example, who are lawyers or executives-- and there were some such.
My mother's girlfriend who lived with us was a high executive in a very, very big factory who was also traveling all over Europe before the war.
She was doing the same thing with my mother.
And they were making those application for a few zlotys.
And it was completely different.
We were-- we felt-- like the Germans said, like [GERMAN].
It was sub-human, sort of.
And that-- that was terrible.
I-- I felt very strange when I was thinking about it.
When I was studying, one of my mother's girlfriend who lived with us, she was very fluent in French.
And I was taking French in gymnasium, so she was continuing teaching me French.
And another friend of my mother, who had PhD in classics, ancient history, and philosophy, she was teaching me still Latin so I wouldn't forget this.
But-- and by myself and with my mother, who was also very good in math, I was still doing my algebra lesson.
And I felt bad about this whole situation.
I was reading a lot.
I took all my books, and my mother's friends had a lot of books.
And so I had plenty of reading because libraries, everything, of course, were closed.
And so I didn't feel that I completely-- at that time, I believed that the war will end soon.
But it didn't.
When they closed the school.
And I saw that my uncle was doing extremely well.
He was still making very good money, and he could stay in German section of the city in his apartment.
I-- and my mother suggested also to me, since her friend was a teacher in the school where they were learning dressmaking.
I went, and few other girls also went to her, and I learned how to do the patterns and how to sew.
And I was very good at that.
My uncle was permitting me to use one of his machine in his place so I could practice this besides doing it in that teacher's house.
And this gave me certain occupation and feeling that I'm doing something with myself and that I learned something useful, and I enjoy this.
We didn't move, as I said, not from grandfather's house.
From grandfather's house, we went to the suburbs, where we stayed.
And over there, I also-- since they were catching people on the street and taking them to work in Germany, there was not enough hands to do.
I was working in the field.
I was also [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] taking care of the chickens and geese.
Also I was milking the cows.
And I learned to do this sort of thing.
And in a way, I really enjoyed that.
Well, it-- it gave me something to do.
So-- so long we were over there, we felt not that bad because we were-- first of all, we were among all kind of people.
And secondly, it's-- it was useful.
My-- my mother and her two sisters were going also in the field when there was harvest and to work.
It was still sort of semi-normal life.
And then from there, we went to ghetto.
Ghetto was already established about a year when we came to ghetto.
Ghetto was always closed.
Some people had permission to go to the city.
But basically, it was closed.
However, what happened was that the street cars, they called tram, was going through ghetto to go to the gentile part.
So very frequently, for example, young children were going in the tram, and then they were jumping off.
They were bringing some food and selling it.
So this was going on.
The tram was slowing down when it was going to ghetto, so they could jump off.
And then when it was going back, jump back in.
This happened all the time.
And also, when I was in ghetto, I got frequently pass that I could come to the city.
But of course, at that time, we were wearing what they called armband.
It was white with the blue Jewish star.
Looked like the flag of Israel now.
And we didn't have the yellow stars like they had in some other cities in Germany, just this band.
Now, sometimes, since allegedly, I didn't look Jewish, and the Poles could recognize a Jew immediately, I was taking it off.
Which of course, I was risking my life.
But I was doing it when I was going to my girlfriend's house.
Oh, you had to go and ask for it.
And--
From the German authorities over there.
And you know, when you came in and were trying to do nicely, when the guy likes you-- liked you, he said, OK.
You have it, and you can go today.
That was usually for several hours in one day once in a while.
And that was it.
Well, this was always a problem because the ghetto did not expand.
Actually, it was later on, they were shrinking it.
And so more people and more people were coming to the apartments.
And this created problems.
Somehow, we were very lucky because we got some people.
We got a family.
Young boy my age-- they were German Jews-- with his mother, who was a nurse.
So they came in.
And later on, believe it or not, there was a family with two children.
And somehow, we got together, and we said we have to make the best of it.
Let's enjoy it.
At evening, let's play cards.
I was assigned to teach the children.
The boy was, I think, six years old, and his sister was four.
I was teaching them how to read and write in Polish.
They were German Jews, and they brought them.
They were in different city.
And so but in some places, just too many people.
They were yelling and screaming.
I didn't know how to cook.
We always had a cook before the war.
Neither my mothers, so this lady, who was a extremely good cook, said, do what I'm doing, and you're gonna learn.
And I learned from her.
So we actually had-- And my grandfather, who lived in another place, he and my two aunts had actually whole room for themselves.
So they put a little electric kitchen there, although they couldn't use too much electricity because they could use only certain amount.
But they were able to cook a little bit.
So it was not really that bad.
So long we were in ghetto, the-- the life in Cracow ghetto-- there were people who were very poor.
And my aunt was cooking a lot of soup and giving out to people.
And I know that my husband's mother was having huge kettles and was-- they were financially doing OK, because believe it or not, their business before the war was just the place where was ghetto.
So they still continued to have their business.
And so they were making some money.
So they were doing not as bad as other people, and they could help others.
People were very-- actually, most of the people were helpful.
What type of actions were taken in the ghetto?
Well, at nighttime mainly, when the Jewish police knew that somebody was well-to-do, they were taking them to Auschwitz, and they were taking their money and whatever they had over there.
That was in the beginning.
And then, of course, started, in 1942, the final solution.
Which they call it [GERMAN].
Then they really take it.
And we all believed that they were going someplace to Ukraine, and they working over there.
And we did not-- although they were saying that there were those supports for [GERMAN], namely places where they were killing them, we didn't believe this.
We couldn't possibly.
And it was quite some time till we realized that the people were going to their death.
Yes.
Judenrat, yes.
In a way.
Now, very funny thing.
The-- the commissar, the-- the biggest man over there, Mr.
Smha Scpera, may he burn in hell, he completely identified himself with the German.
Before the war, he was a clerk in the [HEBREW], namely the Jewish organization.
I remember he was coming to our house to collect the dues from grandfather.
And grandfather was very well known and big businessman, wealthy.
The guy, when was coming out from our apartment, he was going backwards and bowing.
[INAUDIBLE] And now he became a big man, and he collaborated with the Germans.
And some of the people, the so-called [GERMAN], OD, they were also collaborating.
Not all of them, but they learned-- you know, this is a very known psychological fact.
You look up to somebody, and they were-- some of them were oppressing.
I received many beatings, but the worst one was from the Jew.
He's alive some place in Australia.
I should have-- if I would have been like I should have been, he would have hanged after the war in Poland, because I met him, and he deserved it.
But then I didn't want to have blood on my hands.
But some of the [POLISH] behaved in German manner.
They were just-- I guess-- It's strange, but-- and very-- and very little is actually said about it.
And many of them, especially in Cracow, were killed.
They thought that they will survive, but they knew too much, as far as the German were concerned.
So they all died.
And that was-- Unfortunately, my mother's friend's husband, who was a doctor, he was doctor in our concentration camp.
He was also by Schindler.
And he came to Cracow after the war.
And the next day, he was put in prison and-- and he was hanged.
Many of them were hanged, and many run away.
If they were smart, they didn't go.
Schindler, had he come to Cracow, would have hanged.
The reason was that there were people who were taken off the list.
And therefore, if they survive, they were not singing his praises.
Because for some people, he was their rescuer.
He was terrific.
But for others, he was the killer.
Oh, I knew Schindler since the very first day he came to Cracow.
The Germans who came in the beginning were placed in elegant Jewish apartment.
And he was assigned to my uncle.
He got a room there.
Because my aunt was very fluent in German because she went to school in Silesia, where they were-- from the first grade, there was Polish and German.
And he saw many books.
He became very friendly with my uncle.
He was a poor man when he came in, and he came without his wife.
And his-- Germans were coming in.
If they have some-- knew someone, were becoming so-called [GERMAN], Jewish property.
Namely, they were taking over businesses and houses, getting the money.
If they were nice, they were giving some little money to the Jews.
If they were not nice, nothing.
And Schindler, after he became well-acquainted with my uncle, he asked him, do you know somebody who would like to have a good [GERMAN]?
I will be very good.
And my uncle said, well, I play cards.
He was an avid bridge player.
I play cards with some very wealthy people.
I'm going to talk to them.
And he talked to the guy from Amalia.
And Schindler and that guy met in my uncle's house, and they made a deal.
And Schindler took over Amalia.
The factory, yes.
They were-- you know, it's called Amalia because they were making those pots and pans which were-- had this Amalia, they called, in-- inside and outside.
And he-- later on, when people went to concentration camp, he built barracks over there, and he took those people in them.
Incidentally, my husband's cousin and her mother were in Schindler's thing.
But when later on, when they all had to come to concentration camp, they were, of course, taken off the list.
They survive.
The-- his cousin's mother, his aunt, died already.
But the cousin is alive over there.
She's in Israel.
She lives in Netanya.
Easily.
You see, Schindler wanted my uncle to be in Amalia.
But my uncle got a better deal.
Since he was working for Hans Frank, he got an apartment in [POLISH].
He was traveling from over there.
He didn't have to live in ghetto and in camp, so he preferred this.
But were came there so-called what they called action, they took him in.
And later on came an order to remove him from the cattle train, and they couldn't find him and his family.
Had he gone with Schindler, like Schindler asked him to.
Now, I could have easily gone to Schindler and say, take me, because he knew me.
But my mother said we cannot do it because in order to put us on the list, they have to take somebody else off.
And that's not how we want to survive.
And that was it.
